Para 7.7 concerns reporting regulatory obligations — not engaged here.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Model articles and articles of association
Business Law & Practice.stem: Wharton Magna Tech Ltd is incorporated without filing bespoke articles. Which articles apply?

options.A: The Model Articles for Private Companies Limited by Shares (CA 2006 s.20) apply automatically.
options.B: The company has no articles until it adopts them at its first general meeting.
options.C: Table A of the Companies Act 1985 applies to all companies incorporated after 2006.
options.D: The Companies Act 2006 itself substitutes for articles.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.20 provides that if a company is registered without articles, the relevant model articles (prescribed by the Secretary of State) apply. For a private company limited by shares incorporated without bespoke articles, the Model Articles for Private Companies Limited by Shares (set out in the Companies (Model Articles) Regulations 2008) apply in their entirety. Table A (C) was the predecessor under CA 1985 and does not apply to companies incorporated under CA 2006. Option B is wrong — the model articles fill the gap automatically. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth was a director of three companies that became insolvent over five years. An insolvency practitioner reports his conduct to the Insolvency Service. Under the Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986, what is the maximum period of disqualification?

options.A: 5 years.
options.B: 10 years.
options.C: 15 years.
options.D: Lifetime ban.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under the Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986 s.6, a court must make a disqualification order against a director of an insolvent company whose conduct makes them unfit to be concerned in the management of a company. The minimum period is 2 years and the maximum is 15 years. For the most serious cases (e.g., fraud), the court has power under s.2 to disqualify for up to 15 years. There is no lifetime ban in standard CDDA proceedings. Options A, C and D state incorrect maximum periods.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd has retained earnings of £200,000 but its most recent accounts show a current year loss of £80,000, leaving distributable profits of £120,000. The board declares a dividend of £150,000. Is this lawful?

options.A: Yes — the company has retained earnings and the directors have discretion to declare dividends from reserves.
options.B: Yes — current year losses do not affect the ability to pay dividends from retained earnings.
options.C: No — under CA 2006 s.830, a company may only make a distribution out of profits available for the purpose; the available distributable profits are £120,000 and a dividend of £150,000 would exceed them.
options.D: No — dividends can only be paid from profits made in the current financial year.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.830 restricts distributions to 'profits available for the purpose', defined as accumulated realised profits less accumulated realised losses (including current year losses). Here: £200,000 retained earnings less £80,000 current year loss = £120,000 distributable profits. A dividend of £150,000 exceeds this amount and would be unlawful. Option A is wrong — directors do not have unfettered discretion to distribute from gross reserves without accounting for losses. Option B is wrong — current year losses reduce distributable profits. Option D is wrong in the other direction — retained earnings can be distributed.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Victoria Ashworth, holding 15% of shares in Wharton Magna Tech Ltd, believes that James Ashworth (a director) has diverted a corporate opportunity to himself in breach of fiduciary duty, causing loss to the company. The board refuses to sue James. What is Victoria's option and what court permission is required?

options.A: Victoria can sue James directly for the loss to her shareholding value.
options.B: Victoria can only act if she holds more than 25% of shares.
options.C: Victoria may bring a derivative claim on behalf of the company under CA 2006 s.260, but she must first obtain permission from the court, which will consider factors including the good faith of the member and whether the cause of action is prima facie meritorious.
options.D: The board's refusal is final — shareholders cannot override it.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.260 allows a member to bring a derivative claim on behalf of the company in respect of a cause of action vested in the company (e.g., breach of directors' duties). Section 261 requires the claimant to apply to the court for permission to continue the claim. The court must dismiss the claim at the permission stage unless the application discloses a prima facie case; it then considers factors including: the good faith of the member, whether the act is capable of authorisation or ratification, and whether the company's interests indicate continuation. Option A is wrong — the loss is to the company, not directly to shareholders. Option B is wrong — there is no minimum shareholding threshold for s.260. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: The shareholders of Hollowell Engineering Ltd pass a special resolution to wind up the company voluntarily. The directors cannot make a declaration of solvency. What type of winding-up will follow?

options.A: Members' voluntary liquidation.
options.B: Compulsory liquidation — only the court can wind up an insolvent company.
options.C: Creditors' voluntary liquidation — where directors cannot make a statutory declaration of solvency, the winding-up proceeds as a creditors' voluntary liquidation under IA 1986 s.90.
options.D: Administration — the company must enter administration before any voluntary liquidation.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: IA 1986 s.90 provides that where a company resolves to wind up voluntarily but the directors are unable to make a statutory declaration of solvency (as required for a members' voluntary liquidation under s.89), the winding-up proceeds as a creditors' voluntary liquidation. In a CVL, creditors have the right to nominate and appoint the liquidator and to form a liquidation committee. Option A (MVL) requires a declaration of solvency by a majority of directors. Option B is wrong — voluntary liquidation does not require court involvement if a special resolution is passed. Option D is wrong — administration is a separate rescue/realisation procedure.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Donna Pearce has a contract of employment with six months' notice. Northgate Couriers dismisses her without notice or pay in lieu. She has 18 months' continuous service. Which claim(s) can she bring?

options.A: Unfair dismissal only — wrongful dismissal is not available to employees.
options.B: Both wrongful dismissal and unfair dismissal.
options.C: Wrongful dismissal (breach of contract claim for loss of notice pay) but not unfair dismissal, as she lacks two years' qualifying service.
options.D: Unfair dismissal only, as she has been dismissed without fair procedure.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Wrongful dismissal is a common law claim for breach of the notice term in the employment contract. There is no qualifying period — it is available from day one of employment. Pearce is owed six months' notice and receives none, so she has a wrongful dismissal claim for six months' pay (less mitigation). However, unfair dismissal under ERA 1996 s.94 requires two years' continuous employment (s.108). With only 18 months, she cannot bring an unfair dismissal claim (absent automatic unfair dismissal grounds, which are not on these facts). Option A is wrong — wrongful dismissal is a well-established common law action. Option B would require two years' service for unfair dismissal.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Priya Mehta applies for a promotion at Hollowell Engineering. She is not shortlisted. The decision-maker later says he did not think she would be 'committed enough' after recently returning from maternity leave. Which protected characteristic is directly engaged?

options.A: Religion or belief.
options.B: Age.
options.C: Sex — unfavourable treatment related to pregnancy/maternity return is direct sex discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 s.13, or may constitute pregnancy and maternity discrimination under s.18.
options.D: Disability.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under the Equality Act 2010, pregnancy and maternity is a protected characteristic (s.4 and s.18). Additionally, treating a woman less favourably because she has taken maternity leave amounts to direct sex discrimination (s.13). The decision-maker's comment about 'commitment' following maternity leave directly links the unfavourable treatment to the protected characteristic. None of the other protected characteristics listed (religion/belief, age, disability) are engaged on these facts.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Colonel Ashworth has retired as a formal director of Ashworth Developments Ltd but continues to instruct the remaining directors on all major financial decisions, which they routinely follow. Is Ashworth a shadow director?

options.A: No — a person who has retired as director cannot be a shadow director.
options.B: No — shadow directors only arise in insolvency situations.
options.C: Yes — CA 2006 s.251 defines a shadow director as a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of a company are accustomed to act; Ashworth satisfies this test.
options.D: Yes — but only if he holds shares in the company.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.251(1) defines a shadow director as a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of the company are accustomed to act. Former director status does not prevent a person from being a shadow director (A is wrong). The definition applies generally, not just in insolvency (C is wrong — though shadow director status has specific relevance in insolvency proceedings under CDDA 1986 and IA 1986). Shareholding (D) is irrelevant. On these facts, Ashworth's instructions are routinely followed — he fits squarely within s.251.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Income tax — employment income vs self-employment
Business Law & Practice.stem: Clive Dring provides his bakery services to various customers but operates entirely through a personal service company, Dring Bakes Ltd, of which he is the sole director and shareholder. The bakery's main client pays Dring Bakes Ltd rather than Dring personally. HMRC wishes to determine whether IR35 applies. What is the key question?

options.A: Whether Dring Bakes Ltd is incorporated in England and Wales.
options.B: Whether Dring has registered for VAT.
options.C: Whether, if the personal service company were disregarded, the relationship between Dring personally and the end client would be one of employment (the IR35 / off-payroll working rules in ITEPA 2003 Chapter 8/Chapter 10).
options.D: Whether Dring Bakes Ltd has more than one client.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: The IR35 rules (ITEPA 2003 Chapter 8 for private sector from April 2021, Chapter 10) address the situation where an individual provides personal services through an intermediary company. The key question is the 'deemed employment' test: if the personal service company were ignored and the individual contracted directly with the end client, would they be an employee? Factors include control, substitution, and integration. Incorporation jurisdiction (A) is irrelevant. VAT registration (C) is a separate matter. Having multiple clients (D) is one relevant factor in the status assessment but is not the key question for IR35 specifically.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Stamp Duty Land Tax — residential threshold
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hassan Nazari purchases a residential property in England for £300,000. He is a first-time buyer. What SDLT relief, if any, applies and at what threshold?

options.A: No relief — all residential purchases are subject to standard SDLT rates.
options.B: First-time buyer relief sets the threshold at £300,000; Nazari pays nothing.
options.C: First-time buyer relief applies: no SDLT on the first £425,000 of the purchase price (for properties up to £625,000); 5% on the remainder up to £625,000. The full purchase price is below £425,000 so no SDLT is payable.
options.D: First-time buyer relief is only available for new-build properties.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: First-time buyer relief (FA 2003 s.57AA, as amended) provides that a first-time buyer purchasing a residential property in England pays no SDLT on the first £425,000 of the purchase price, provided the total purchase price does not exceed £625,000. As the purchase price here is £300,000 (which is below the £425,000 threshold), no SDLT is payable. Option B states the old threshold (£300,000 was the threshold before 23 September 2022 and after the temporary increase ended in March 2025 — the current post-September 2022 threshold is £425,000 while the stamp duty cut was in effect). Option D is wrong — the relief applies to all qualifying residential purchases, not just new-build.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Wharton Magna Tech Ltd qualifies as a small company under CA 2006. What is its main advantage regarding audit obligations?

options.A: It must have an audit but can file abbreviated accounts.
options.B: It may file accounts 18 months after year-end instead of 9 months.
options.C: It is exempt from the requirement to have its accounts audited (CA 2006 s.477), unless the company is part of a group, a public company, or within certain regulated sectors.
options.D: It is not required to prepare accounts at all.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.477 provides that a company that qualifies as small in respect of a financial year is exempt from the requirement to have its annual accounts audited. To qualify as small, a company must satisfy two of three criteria: turnover ≤ £10.2m, balance sheet total ≤ £5.1m, employees ≤ 50. The exemption does not apply if the company is a public company, an authorised insurance company, a banking company, a member of an ineligible group, etc. A small company that is a subsidiary also loses the exemption unless the parent provides a guarantee under s.479A. Option A is wrong — the whole point of small company status is avoiding audit. Option B is wrong — the filing deadline for small companies is 9 months for private companies. Option D is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd changes its registered office from London to Wharton Magna (Northamptonshire). What must it do to effect this change?

options.A: Pass a special resolution of shareholders.
options.B: Notify all known creditors individually.
options.C: File notice of the change with Companies House using Form AD01; the change takes effect when the Registrar registers it (CA 2006 s.87).
options.D: Obtain court approval for the change of registered office.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under CA 2006 s.87, a company may change its registered office by giving notice to the Registrar using the prescribed form (AD01). The change takes effect when the Registrar registers it. No shareholder resolution (A) is required. Notification to creditors (C) is not a legal requirement (though it may be practical for service of documents purposes). Court approval (D) is not required. The company must ensure its registered office remains in the same country as its place of registration (England, Scotland or Wales) — moving from London to Northamptonshire stays within England, so no issue arises.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma joins Crane & Pelletier as a new partner. The firm has a pre-existing loan from a bank. Is Sharma personally liable for this loan?

options.A: Yes — partners are jointly and severally liable for all firm debts whenever incurred.
options.B: Yes — Sharma is liable because he took on assets and liabilities equally with existing partners.
options.C: No — under PA 1890 s.17, a person who is admitted as a partner into an existing firm does not thereby become liable to the creditors of the firm for anything done before he became a partner.
options.D: Yes — unless the bank expressly releases the old partners from liability.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Partnership Act 1890 s.17(1) provides that a person admitted as a partner into an existing firm does not thereby become liable to the creditors of the firm for anything done before he became a partner. This is the rule against retrospective liability for incoming partners. Options A and C overstate the position. Option D describes novation (where the bank releases the old partners and accepts the new partner) — this is a separate process and not a condition of the s.17 protection.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd proposes to allot new shares for cash. Victoria Ashworth holds 20% of the existing shares. What right does she have before the allotment?

options.A: No right — pre-emption rights only arise on a transfer of existing shares.
options.B: A right of pre-emption only if this is written into the company's articles.
options.C: A right to block the allotment by special resolution.
options.D: A right of pre-emption under CA 2006 s.561: existing shareholders must be offered the new shares pro rata to their existing holdings before the shares are offered to outside parties; Victoria is entitled to 20% of the new issue.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.561 imposes a statutory right of pre-emption on a company proposing to allot equity securities wholly for cash: existing shareholders must be offered the new shares on the same terms, pro rata to their existing holdings, before the shares are offered to anyone else. Victoria holds 20% and must be offered 20% of any new issue. The right can be disapplied by special resolution under s.570/571. Option A confuses pre-emption on allotment with pre-emption on transfer (often found in articles). Option C — shareholders cannot block allotment by special resolution alone if authority already exists. Option B is wrong — the s.561 right arises by statute, not only from the articles.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd wishes to borrow £50 million for a major development. No single bank is willing to lend the full amount. What financing structure is most appropriate?

options.A: A series of separate bilateral loans from multiple banks.
options.B: A revolving credit facility from a single bank.
options.C: A bond issue on the London Stock Exchange.
options.D: A syndicated loan — a loan in which a group of lenders (the 'syndicate') provide funds to a single borrower, arranged and administered by an agent bank; the borrower has one loan agreement but multiple lenders.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A syndicated loan allows multiple banks (the syndicate) to participate in a single loan to one borrower. The loan is governed by a single facility agreement, minimising documentation and simplifying administration for the borrower (who deals through an agent bank). This is the standard market solution where a loan is too large for one lender. Option A (separate bilateral loans) would create multiple agreements with different terms, greater complexity, and co-ordination problems. A bond issue (C) is a capital markets solution with different regulatory and documentation requirements. A revolving credit facility (D) is typically from a single bank and serves working capital rather than large project financing needs.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd proposes to acquire a commercial property worth £800,000 from its director, Colonel Ashworth. The company's net assets are £3,000,000. What shareholder approval (if any) is required?

options.A: No approval required — director transactions are approved by the board.
options.B: Ordinary shareholder resolution only — because the property value exceeds the lower threshold.
options.C: Ordinary shareholder resolution under CA 2006 s.190 — a company must not enter into an arrangement with a director involving the acquisition of a substantial non-cash asset without member approval; £800,000 exceeds the greater of £100,000 and 10% of net assets (£300,000), so it is a substantial property transaction.
options.D: Special resolution (75% majority) required.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.190 provides that a company must not enter into an arrangement under which a director (or person connected with a director) acquires from or transfers to the company a substantial non-cash asset without prior approval by ordinary resolution of the members. A non-cash asset is 'substantial' if its value exceeds the lesser of £100,000 or 10% of net asset value — with a lower threshold of £5,000. Here: 10% × £3,000,000 = £300,000. The property is worth £800,000 — this exceeds both £100,000 and £300,000, making it a substantial non-cash asset transaction requiring member approval. An ordinary resolution (simple majority) suffices — not a special resolution (D). Option A is wrong.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers requires its dispatch drivers to work an average of 52 hours per week. A driver, Owen Davies, has not signed a written opt-out agreement. Is this lawful?

options.A: Yes — the 48-hour limit only applies to office workers.
options.B: No — but Northgate can require up to 60 hours if it pays overtime.
options.C: Yes — the 48-hour limit can be exceeded with verbal agreement of the employee.
options.D: No — the Working Time Regulations 1998 reg.4 limits average working time to 48 hours per week over a 17-week reference period; without a valid written opt-out, requiring Davies to work 52 hours on average is unlawful.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Working Time Regulations 1998 reg.4 provides that an employer shall take all reasonable steps to ensure that a worker's average working time (calculated over a 17-week reference period, or up to 52 weeks for certain categories) does not exceed 48 hours per week. An individual can opt out of this limit by written agreement (reg.5). Without a written opt-out, the 48-hour limit applies. A verbal agreement (C) is insufficient — the opt-out must be in writing. The limit applies across all sectors including transport (though specific transport sector rules may modify other aspects). Overtime pay (D) does not override the legal maximum.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: A creditor owed £1,200 by Wharton Magna Tech Ltd presents a winding-up petition to the court on the ground that the company is unable to pay its debts. The company disputes the debt. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The court must wind up the company because the debt exceeds £750.
options.B: The petitioning creditor does not need to prove the debt — the company must disprove it.
options.C: The court will grant a winding-up order only if the debt exceeds £10,000.
options.D: The court has discretion and will consider whether the debt is genuinely disputed on substantial grounds; if it is, the court may dismiss or stay the petition.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A creditor may petition for compulsory liquidation on the ground that the company is unable to pay its debts (IA 1986 s.122(1)(f)). The statutory demand threshold is £750 (s.123(1)(a)). However, the court has discretion under s.125 whether to make a winding-up order. Where the debt is genuinely disputed on substantial grounds, the court will typically dismiss or stay the petition — a winding-up petition is not a debt collection mechanism and using it to collect a disputed debt is an abuse of process (Re a Company [1973]). Option A is wrong — the threshold is met but winding-up is not automatic. Option C states an incorrect threshold. Option B is wrong — the creditor must establish the debt is owing.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duties when acting for lender and borrower
Business Law & Practice.stem: Diana Pelletier acts for both Hollowell Engineering Ltd (the borrower) and its bank (the lender) in connection with a commercial mortgage. During the transaction, she discovers that Hollowell has withheld information about an existing undisclosed charge that would be material to the bank's decision. What must Pelletier do?

options.A: She must keep the information confidential to Hollowell and continue acting for both parties.
options.B: She can continue acting if she obtains Hollowell's consent to disclose.
options.C: She must disclose the undisclosed charge to the bank immediately without informing Hollowell.
options.D: She must cease acting for at least one party; she cannot use Hollowell's confidential information to assist the bank, but she also cannot withhold material information from the lender whose interests she represents — she should withdraw from acting for the bank (and possibly both) and advise the bank to seek independent advice.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: When a solicitor holds confidential information relevant to another client in the same matter, this creates a conflict of interest and a confidentiality problem. SRA Code para 6.4 prohibits acting where confidential information of one client is material to another and cannot be disclosed. Pelletier cannot use Hollowell's confidential information against Hollowell, but she also cannot allow the bank to proceed on a false basis. The correct course is to cease acting for at least the bank (given she cannot fulfil her duty to the bank while bound by Hollowell's confidentiality) and advise the bank to obtain independent advice. Option A permits concealment, breaching duties to the bank. Option C would breach duties to Hollowell without consent. Option B may not resolve the conflict if Hollowell refuses consent.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma, on behalf of Crane & Pelletier, gives a written undertaking to the bank that the firm will hold the title deeds to a property to the bank's order until completion of a loan transaction. Sharma then releases the deeds to the client early without the bank's consent. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Sharma's undertaking is not binding on the firm because he is not a partner.
options.B: Sharma can revoke the undertaking at any time before completion.
options.C: The undertaking is binding only if it was countersigned by the bank.
options.D: A solicitor's undertaking is a personal and professional obligation; breaching it is a serious breach of SRA Code para 1.3 and the firm (as well as Sharma personally) may be required by the court or SRA to remedy the breach or compensate the bank.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A solicitor's undertaking is a personal and professional commitment which is binding from the moment it is given. SRA Code para 1.3 requires solicitors to perform undertakings given. Breach of undertaking is a serious professional matter — the court has jurisdiction to compel performance, and the SRA may impose sanctions. An undertaking is binding even if not countersigned by the recipient (C is wrong). It cannot be revoked unilaterally (D is wrong). As a solicitor employed by the firm, Sharma's professional obligation binds him and, through general agency and vicarious liability principles, also exposes the firm (A is wrong).

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Sole trader vs limited company — liability
Business Law & Practice.stem: Pat Hollowell trades as a sole trader (Hollowell Engineering). A customer sues for breach of contract and obtains judgment for £200,000. Hollowell cannot pay from business assets. Which statement correctly describes his personal exposure?

options.A: Hollowell is not personally liable because trade debts belong to the business.
options.B: Hollowell can convert to a limited company retroactively to limit his exposure.
options.C: Hollowell's liability is capped at the value of the business assets at the time of the contract.
options.D: As a sole trader, Hollowell has unlimited personal liability; his personal assets (house, savings, etc.) are available to satisfy the judgment debt.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A sole trader has no separate legal personality from the business — the business is simply the individual trading. Consequently, a sole trader has unlimited personal liability for all business debts and obligations. There is no asset protection separating business and personal wealth. Option A is wrong — there is no legal distinction between the trader and the business. Option C is wrong — no such cap exists. Option B is wrong — incorporating after the fact does not alter pre-existing personal liability.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd has a financial year ending 31 December 2023. By when must it file its accounts with Companies House?

options.A: Within 6 months of the financial year end — by 30 June 2024.
options.B: Within 21 months of incorporation.
options.C: Within 12 months of the financial year end — by 31 December 2024.
options.D: Within 9 months of the financial year end — by 30 September 2024.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Under CA 2006 s.442, a private company must deliver its annual accounts and reports to Companies House within 9 months of the end of the relevant accounting reference period. For a year ending 31 December 2023, the deadline is 30 September 2024. Public companies have a shorter period of 6 months. Option A (6 months) is the public company deadline. Option C (12 months) is incorrect. Option B (21 months) applies to the first set of accounts after incorporation only, and is now largely superseded.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Owen Davies, aged 22, works as a courier for Northgate Couriers. His employer pays him £9.50 per hour. The National Living Wage for workers aged 21 and over is £11.44 per hour (from April 2024). Is Northgate compliant?

options.A: Yes — the NMW applies only to workers aged 25 and over.
options.B: No — but the remedy is limited to the difference in pay going forward.
options.C: Yes — the National Living Wage only applies to full-time employees.
options.D: No — from April 2024, the National Living Wage of £11.44 per hour applies to workers aged 21 and over; paying £9.50 is unlawful and exposes Northgate to enforcement action by HMRC.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: From 1 April 2024, the National Living Wage (the compulsory minimum wage for workers aged 21 and over) increased to £11.44 per hour. The age threshold was reduced from 23 to 21. As Davies is 22, the NLW applies to him. Paying £9.50 is non-compliant. HMRC can enforce arrears of NMW/NLW, issue penalty notices, and name employers. Option A states the old age threshold (25). Option C is wrong — the NMW/NLW applies to workers, not only full-time employees. Option B understates the remedy — HMRC can require full back-payment of arrears.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: The liquidator of Hollowell Engineering Ltd discovers that shortly before insolvency the company sold its delivery vehicles to a third party at half their market value. Which power allows the liquidator to seek to recover value for creditors?

options.A: Section 214 IA 1986 (wrongful trading).
options.B: Section 127 IA 1986 (void dispositions after commencement of winding-up).
options.C: Section 239 IA 1986 (preference).
options.D: Section 238 IA 1986 (transaction at an undervalue) — the liquidator may apply to the court to set aside a transaction entered into at an undervalue within two years of the onset of insolvency.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: Section 238 IA 1986 allows a liquidator (or administrator) to apply to the court to set aside a transaction at an undervalue — where the company received significantly less consideration than the value of what it transferred. The relevant period is two years ending with the onset of insolvency. Selling vehicles at half their market value is a paradigm s.238 transaction. Section 239 (C) applies to preferences (paying one creditor ahead of others). Section 214 (A) addresses director conduct. Section 127 (D) renders void dispositions of company property made after the commencement of winding-up (i.e., once a petition has been presented), not pre-insolvency dispositions.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Bonus issues and rights issues distinguished
Business Law & Practice.stem: Northgate Couriers Ltd proposes a 1-for-2 rights issue at £1.50 per share (the current market price is £2.00). Which statement about a rights issue is correct?

options.A: A rights issue is a free allocation of shares to existing shareholders from reserves.
options.B: A rights issue requires the disapplication of pre-emption rights.
options.C: A rights issue dilutes existing shareholders without any compensating right.
options.D: A rights issue is an offer of new shares to existing shareholders at a discount to market price, in proportion to their existing holdings, for cash consideration.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: A rights issue is an offer to existing shareholders to subscribe for new shares at a price below market value, in proportion to their existing holdings, for cash. It raises new capital for the company. Shareholders can take up their rights, sell the nil-paid rights, or let them lapse. A bonus (capitalisation) issue (A) is the conversion of reserves into share capital by allocating free shares — no cash is received. A rights issue protects existing shareholders through the pre-emption mechanism (D is wrong — the pre-emption right under s.561 CA 2006 is precisely what is exercised in a rights issue; disapplication would allow a non-pre-emptive issue). Option C is incorrect — shareholders receive the right to subscribe at a discount, which has value.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ashworth Developments Ltd proposes to lend £60,000 to Victoria Ashworth, a director. What approval is required?

options.A: No approval required if the loan is at a commercial rate.
options.B: Approval is required only if the loan exceeds £250,000.
options.C: Board resolution only, passed without Victoria's participation.
options.D: Ordinary shareholder resolution — CA 2006 s.197 requires member approval before a company makes a loan to a director; without approval the loan is voidable.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.197 provides that a company may not make a loan to a director of the company or of its holding company unless the transaction has been approved by an ordinary resolution of the company's members. The approval must be obtained before the loan is made. Contravention renders the transaction voidable by the company and the director (and any connected persons) who authorised it are liable to account for any direct or indirect gain and to indemnify the company (s.213). There is no de minimis exception based on loan amount for directors (s.207 allows a small loans exception of up to £10,000 — not applicable here). Options A, C, and D are all incorrect.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Duty to report — serious misconduct by colleague
Business Law & Practice.stem: Ravi Sharma discovers that his colleague at Crane & Pelletier has been submitting inflated bills to clients and pocketing the difference. Sharma is unsure whether to report this. What is his professional obligation under the SRA Code?

options.A: Sharma should raise the matter with the compliance officer for legal practice (COLP) or report directly to the SRA if necessary; he must not ignore serious misconduct by a colleague (SRA Code para 7.7 and 7.9).
options.B: Sharma has no obligation to report conduct by a colleague — this is the firm's management responsibility.
options.C: Sharma should inform the affected clients immediately.
options.D: Sharma should report only if the misconduct is criminal.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: SRA Code para 7.7 requires a solicitor to report promptly to the SRA or another approved regulator if they have reasonable grounds to believe that another person regulated by the SRA has committed a serious breach. Para 7.9 requires reporting where a person's behaviour poses a risk to clients or others. Inflating bills and misappropriating client funds is a serious breach engaging both obligations. The COLP is the first port of call within the firm, but if the firm will not act, direct reporting to the SRA is required. Option B is wrong — individual solicitors have personal reporting obligations. Option C is not the primary obligation. Option D is wrong — the obligation is not limited to criminal conduct.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Crane & Pelletier has been advising Northgate Couriers Ltd on a dispute for six months. The initial costs estimate was £20,000. Costs have now reached £35,000 and are continuing to increase significantly. Diana Pelletier takes no further action on costs. Which SRA Code obligation may she be breaching?

options.A: The duty to maintain client confidentiality.
options.B: The duty to report to the SRA.
options.C: The duty to avoid conflicts of interest.
options.D: The ongoing duty to give clients the best information they reasonably can about the likely overall cost, including updating them when costs materially exceed earlier estimates (SRA Code para 8.6).

Business Law & Practice.explanation: SRA Code para 8.6 imposes an ongoing obligation on solicitors to give clients the best information they reasonably can about the likely overall cost of the matter. This includes updating clients when costs are likely to significantly exceed any earlier estimate or when there is a material change in circumstances affecting costs. Failing to update Northgate when costs have nearly doubled breaches this continuing obligation. The other options are not engaged by a failure to update cost estimates.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.subtopic: Ratification of breach of duty — s.239 CA 2006
Business Law & Practice.stem: James Ashworth breached his duty under s.175 CA 2006 (conflict of interest) by taking a corporate opportunity for himself. The board and fellow shareholders of Ashworth Developments Ltd wish to ratify his conduct. What is required?

options.A: A special resolution (75% majority) of all shareholders.
options.B: Court approval.
options.C: Unanimous shareholder consent.
options.D: An ordinary resolution of shareholders under CA 2006 s.239; however, the votes of James Ashworth and any member connected with him must be disregarded.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.239 provides the mechanism for ratifying conduct constituting negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust by a director. Ratification requires an ordinary resolution (simple majority) of the members. However, s.239(3) and (4) provide that where the resolution is proposed by the company, it is not effective if the only votes cast in favour are the director concerned and any connected persons — their votes are disregarded. An ordinary resolution (not special) suffices (A is wrong). Unanimity (C) is not required. Court approval (D) is not required for ratification under s.239.

Business Law & Practice.subject: Business Law & Practice
Business Law & Practice.stem: Hollowell Engineering Ltd grants a fixed charge over its factory to a private lender. The charge is never registered at Companies House. The company subsequently goes into liquidation. What is the effect of non-registration on the charge?

options.A: The charge is valid but ranks behind registered charges.
options.B: The charge is void only against subsequent secured creditors, not the liquidator.
options.C: The charge is valid if the lender can prove it was created.
options.D: Under CA 2006 s.859H, the charge is void against the liquidator and any creditor of the company; the secured debt becomes immediately repayable.

Business Law & Practice.explanation: CA 2006 s.859H provides that if a charge subject to the registration requirement is not registered within the prescribed period (21 days), the charge is void against the liquidator (or administrator) and any creditor of the company. On a winding-up, the liquidator can treat the unregistered charge as ineffective and distribute the charged asset to unsecured creditors. The debt secured by the charge immediately becomes repayable (the lender becomes an unsecured creditor). Options A, C, and D are all incorrect — failure to register renders the charge void against liquidators and all creditors, not merely subsequent secured creditors.
